title: NOC 72500 – Crane operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Crane Operators (NOC 72500) are the high-precision lifters driving Canada’s vertical expansion and massive infrastructure renewal. As urban centers like Toronto, Vancouver, and Calgary prioritize high-density housing, these professionals are critical for operating tower, mobile, and specialized industrial cranes. Classified as TEER 2, the role in 2026 has become increasingly technological, utilizing remote-operation systems, 3D lift-planning software, and AI-integrated wind and load sensors to maximize safety on complex job sites.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Occupations usually requiring college, apprenticeship, or supervisory experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group725 – Surveyors, extractors and other technical occupations

duties: Pre-Operation Checks: Inspecting cranes and calculating crane capacities and weight to ensure safety before beginning any lift.
Precision Maneuvering: Operating cranes to lift, move, position, or place machinery, equipment, and other large objects at construction or industrial sites.
Technical Communication: Coordinating with ground crews using hand signals or radio communication to ensure precise load placement.
Maintenance: Cleaning and lubricating cranes and performing routine maintenance to ensure equipment reliability.
Safety Protocols: Monitoring 2026-grade wind and load sensors and responding to site emergencies or hazardous conditions immediately.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Apprenticeship: Completion of a one- to three-year apprenticeship program OR industry courses in crane operating are usually required.
Certification: Trade certification is compulsory in the following provinces:Mobile Crane: NS, NB, QC, ON, MB, AB, and BC.Tower Crane: NL, NS, QC, ON, MB, AB, and BC.
Mobile Crane: NS, NB, QC, ON, MB, AB, and BC.
Tower Crane: NL, NS, QC, ON, MB, AB, and BC.
Red Seal: The Interprovincial Standards Red Seal is highly recommended for national mobility and is often a requirement for high-paying union and industrial contracts.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Moderate to Strong, specifically tied to non-residential construction and provincial housing mandates:

Ontario: Very Good prospects. In February 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations for skilled trades-related occupations, including NOC 72500, with scores starting at 50. On March 25, 2026, further invitations were issued for candidates with job offers in regional hubs (Central and Southwestern Ontario) to support massive industrial and infrastructure projects.

British Columbia: High Priority. Crane operators are officially on the BC PNP Priority Construction Occupations List. Candidates with a valid trade certificate from SkilledTradesBC benefit from targeted draws and significantly lower point thresholds for Permanent Residency.

Alberta: Strongest Demand. Alberta offers the highest median wages for crane operators in Canada, with top-tier earners in the energy and mining sectors reaching $52.19/hr. Approximately 95% of workers in this NOC are employed full-time.

National Trend: Over 94.9% of crane operators receive at least one type of non-wage benefit. The market is currently seeing a "Tech Integration" phase, favoring operators who can handle automated and remote-control crane systems.
Why Work in Canada as a Crane Operator?
Lead Large-Scale Projects: Direct the physical assembly of Canada’s future, from Vancouver's Broadway Subway extension to Toronto's multi-billion dollar transit hubs.
Unbeatable Stability: 68% of crane operators work all year round—higher than the construction average—providing consistent financial security.
Elite Compensation: As a TEER 2 trade, your median hourly wage in Canada is $36.54/hr, with senior operators in major industrial settings earning over $115,000 annually.
Priority for PR: In 2026, this NOC remains a core target for the Express Entry "Trade Occupations" category and is a permanent fixture in BC PNP and OINP priority lists.
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer: On February 18, 2026, OINP issued 951 invitations for skilled trades including NOC 72500 with a score of 50+. This remains a primary route for those with an Ontario job offer.
BC PNP Construction Stream: As a Priority Occupation, crane operators benefit from targeted BC draws with lower score thresholds for those with a valid job offer and trade certificate.
Express Entry (Trade Occupations Category): Updated for 2026. Eligible candidates now require 12 months of full-time experience (up from 6 months in 2025) within the last 3 years to receive an ITA.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Crane operators are classified as "High-Priority Essential Infrastructure Workers."
